summary: I purchased a document related to Father Junipero Serra and dedicated it to Haydee Noya. This acquisition was meaningful to me because of Serra's historical significance in California missions. It connected me to the broader history of the missions and their archival materials.

I was actively involved in the Zamorano Club, attending meetings and participating in membership activities. The club was a key part of my engagement with bibliophiles and collectors interested in California and Western Americana. It provided a community for sharing knowledge and resources.

Thomas W. Temple II was a colleague who worked extensively with mission archives. His expertise helped deepen my understanding of mission history and archival practices. We shared interests in preserving and studying these important historical documents.

Father Jose Thompson was connected to the De la Guerra papers, which I had the opportunity to study. His role was significant in preserving these family documents that shed light on California history. This connection enriched my research and collecting activities.

The De la Guerra family papers were an important archival collection I encountered through Father Jose Thompson. These documents provided valuable insights into early California society and missions. I was involved in studying and preserving these papers as part of my work with mission archives.

I participated in the remodeling of the San Juan Capistrano Mission, which was a significant project preserving California's mission heritage. This hands-on involvement connected me directly with the physical history of the missions. It was rewarding to contribute to the restoration of such an iconic site.

The Huntington Library has vast holdings and collections that fascinated me deeply. I was interested in its history and the classification of its materials, which I studied extensively. Eventually, I returned to the U.S. to begin work at the Huntington Library, continuing my academic career there.

In 1945, I was in Germany working in the historical section of the Seventh Army during the closing days of World War II. This experience exposed me to European libraries and bookstores amid the postwar environment. I also visited notable sites like Neuschwanstein and Linderhof during this time.

I served in the historical section of the Seventh United States Army in 1945, working under William B. Goddard. My role involved documenting and preserving historical records during the final phase of World War II in Europe. This military experience was pivotal in shaping my postwar academic and collecting pursuits.

William B. Goddard was my superior in the historical section of the Seventh Army in Germany during 1945. Working with him gave me valuable insight into military historical documentation at the close of World War II. His leadership influenced my approach to archival work and research.

While in Europe at the end of World War II, I had the opportunity to visit Neuschwanstein Castle. This visit was part of my broader exploration of European cultural sites during my military service. The castle's history and architecture left a lasting impression on me.

During my time in Europe in 1945, I also visited Linderhof Palace. Like Neuschwanstein, it was a remarkable example of Bavarian royal architecture that I was fortunate to see firsthand. These visits enriched my appreciation for European history and culture.

My experiences during World War II included serving in the historical section of the Seventh Army in Germany in 1945. The war's end brought me into contact with European libraries, bookstores, and cultural sites. These experiences influenced my later academic and collecting career.

I spent time in Morocco where I engaged in collection activities and met important local families like the Guillens and the Bou Helals in Rabat. Working with Prosper Ricard and Louis Endres, I developed a keen interest in Moroccan bindings. These experiences broadened my understanding of North African cultural heritage.

Gerald McDonald left me a legacy that influenced my collecting interests and career. I inherited materials from him and continued to build on his work, particularly in the field of book collecting. His connections and collections were foundational to my own pursuits.

Meeting the Guillen family in Rabat, Morocco, was a significant part of my collecting activities there. I also encountered them later in Paris, which helped deepen my appreciation for Moroccan cultural artifacts. Their connection was important to my research on Moroccan bindings.

The Bou Helal family in Rabat, Morocco, were key contacts during my collection efforts in the region. Their support and knowledge enriched my understanding of local traditions and book arts. This relationship was an important aspect of my work in North Africa.

Rabat, Morocco, was a central location for my collection activities and meetings with the Guillen and Bou Helal families. The city served as a cultural hub where I could engage with local experts and collectors. My time there was crucial for developing my interest in Moroccan bindings.

I worked alongside Prosper Ricard in Morocco during my collection activities. His expertise and collaboration were valuable as I explored Moroccan bindings and cultural materials. Our partnership helped me gain deeper insights into North African book arts.

Louis Endres was another collaborator during my time in Morocco, assisting in collection efforts focused on local bindings and manuscripts. His involvement complemented my research and collecting interests in the region. Together, we expanded the scope of our cultural acquisitions.

After the war, I attended the University of Paris and took courses at the Institut d'Ethnologie. Paris was also where I met the Guillen family again and deepened my connections with the Resistance through people like Yvonne Oddon and Harper Kelley. The city was a vibrant center for my academic and collecting pursuits.

I attended the University of Paris after World War II, enrolling in courses at the Institut d'Ethnologie. This academic experience enriched my understanding of ethnology and cultural studies. It was an important step in my postwar education and career development.

At the Institut d'Ethnologie in Paris, I took courses that expanded my knowledge of cultural anthropology and ethnology. This institution was central to my academic growth after the war. It also connected me with scholars like Yvonne Oddon and others involved in Resistance history.

I met Yvonne Oddon while studying at the Institut d'Ethnologie in Paris. She was connected to the Resistance, and through her, I gained access to early Resistance memoirs and insights into wartime experiences. Her influence helped shape my interest in collecting these important historical documents.

Harper Kelley was someone I met in Paris who was involved with the Resistance. Living arrangements allowed me to meet members of the Resistance, including Kelley, which enriched my understanding of wartime France. I collected early Resistance memoirs partly due to these connections.

After my postwar experiences in Europe and Morocco, I returned to the United States to continue my academic and professional career. I worked at the Huntington Library and studied under Joseph B. Lockey at UCLA. The U.S. was the base for my library and collecting work.

Joseph B. Lockey was my mentor at UCLA, where I attended seminars that were intellectually rigorous and stimulating. His guidance helped shape my academic development and decision to attend library school. Lockey's seminars involved deep research and discussions that prepared me for my career.

At UCLA, I participated in Lockey's seminars and worked as a research assistant in the Department of History under John Caughey. I also founded the UCLA Anthropological Society around 1937, helping to develop the field on campus. UCLA was central to my academic and professional growth.

John Caughey was my supervisor when I worked as a research assistant in the Department of History at UCLA. His mentorship was important during my early academic career. Working with him complemented my studies and research interests.

I worked at the New York Public Library researching Noah Webster and collecting eighteenth-century Mexican engravings. This position allowed me to deepen my bibliographic knowledge and build connections with colleagues in the field. The library was a key site for my early collecting activities.

At the New York Public Library, I conducted research on Noah Webster, which contributed to my interest in Americana and bibliographic studies. Webster's work was part of the broader context of my collecting and academic pursuits. This research helped refine my collecting criteria.

I collected eighteenth-century Mexican engravings and other Mexican art as part of my bibliographic and cultural interests. These works were among the principal collections I focused on, reflecting my broader interest in regional art and history. My collecting extended beyond books to include visual arts.

I founded the UCLA Anthropological Society around 1937 when anthropology was still developing at the university. We held early meetings, established officers, and organized events like the Pueblo Indian exhibit in 1938. The society helped foster interest and scholarship in anthropology on campus.

In 1938, the UCLA Anthropological Society mounted an exhibit on the Pueblo peoples, which was one of our notable early activities. This exhibit helped raise awareness and appreciation for indigenous cultures in the Southwest. It was part of our efforts to promote anthropology at UCLA.

In 1937, I founded the UCLA Anthropological Society during the early days of anthropology at the university. This year marked the beginning of organized efforts to promote the discipline and build a community of scholars and students. It was a foundational moment for my involvement in anthropology.

In 1938, the UCLA Anthropological Society held its first major event, the Pueblo Indian exhibit. This was a significant milestone in our early activities, helping to establish the society's presence and mission. It reflected our commitment to showcasing indigenous cultures and advancing anthropology.
